idea.hook: Wanna visit our galaxy's supermassive black hole?
facts: The Milky Way's supermassive black hole, Sagittarius A* (Sgr A*), is about 26,000 light-years away from Earth.
Even traveling at the ultimate speed limit – the speed of light (300,000 km/s) – a signal would take 26,000 years to reach Sgr A*.
A hypothetical advanced probe moving at an incredible 0.1% the speed of light (300 km/s) would still need 26 MILLION years for the journey.
idea.payoff: Crossing our own galaxy isn't just a trip; it's an unfathomable odyssey spanning geological and cosmic timescales!
idea.title: Road Trip to Galactic Core? 🤯🌌

scenes.text: Wanna visit our galaxy's supermassive black hole?
scenes.visual: A stylized Milky Way galaxy appears, with a glowing, intense center. Earth's tiny dot is visible somewhere in the spiral arm.
The Milky Way zooms slightly, highlighting Earth and the bright Galactic Center (Sgr A*). A dotted line appears connecting them, with '26,000 Light-Years' appearing along the line.
A bright beam of light travels from Earth towards Sgr A* across the galactic background. A digital clock icon rapidly counts up and settles on '26,000 Years'.

scenes.text: Advanced Probe (0.1% Light Speed): 26 MILLION YEARS!
scenes.visual: A sleek, futuristic probe icon slowly glides across the galactic expanse from Earth towards Sgr A*. A calendar icon flips pages at an incredibly slow rate, ultimately showing '26 Million Years'.

scenes.text: Crossing our galaxy: An unfathomable odyssey spanning cosmic timescales!
scenes.visual: The Milky Way fills the entire screen, swirling majestically. Earth and Sgr A* are no longer distinct, emphasizing the immense scale and time. Stars subtly drift or 'age' in the background, reinforcing 'cosmic timescales'.
